raw.reasoning: Without a date context, '5pm' refers to today (the anchor date) at that time of day.

March 4 without a year, mentioned in January 2026, clearly refers to the upcoming date March 4, 2026.

On October 31, 'the 5th' without a month naturally refers to the upcoming November 5th; the previous 5th (October) has already passed.

Without explicit month/year context, "the 1st" most likely refers to the next approaching 1st of a month (October 1), though it could also mean the most recent 1st (September 1) or some other month depending on prior conversation.
Without a year, 'March 4' most likely refers to the next occurrence (2027) since March 4, 2026 has already passed, but the past year remains a reasonable alternative.

Since the anchor is Tuesday morning at 11 AM, 'Tuesday at 3pm' most naturally refers to later today; however, a listener might reasonably interpret it as next Tuesday if context suggested a future commitment.

"Soon" is one of the most vague temporal expressions; it could mean within hours, days, or weeks, with no clear endpoint.

The long weekend begins Thursday, November 27 (Thanksgiving); 'before' clearly means the period up until then, but lacks a defined start point, making it ambiguous whether it means just today or several days prior.
One month prior to March 12 is clearly February 12; 'about' introduces minor imprecision but a specific point ~30 days ago is the evident intent.

The upcoming summer of 2026 (June through August) is the clear first choice from the current March date, though summer could also refer to the astronomical season (June 21–September 22) or extend into May.

The phrase "around the holidays" typically encompasses the U.S. winter holiday season from Thanksgiving through New Year's, but the exact boundaries are ambiguous—it could start earlier or end later, and may or may not include Thanksgiving itself.
The new year clearly refers to January 1, 2026, the date that marks the start of the next calendar year; some might interpret it as the entire year 2026, but the specific day is the primary meaning.
Mid-month typically refers to the middle period of a month (roughly days 10–20), centered on the 15th, but the precise range is somewhat open to interpretation.
On a Tuesday, 'the weekend' most naturally refers to the upcoming Saturday–Sunday (June 13–14), though there is a small chance the speaker meant the previous weekend.
From Tuesday June 9, 'next Friday' clearly refers to the Friday of the same week (June 12), though a small minority might interpret it as the Friday of the following week.
"Few months" typically covers 2–3 months; interpreting this as the last ~2.5 months from the anchor gives January through mid-March, but the exact starting point could reasonably be December instead.
"Recently" refers to the near past but has no precise boundary; a reasonable default is the last week (~7 days before the anchor), though it could reasonably span anywhere from 3 days to a month.
